feed.title: Our World in Data
feed.subtitle: Research and data to make progress against the world’s largest problems

entry.title: Why did renewables become so cheap so fast? And what can we do to use this global opportunity for green growth?
entry.summary: Fossil fuels dominate the global power supply because until very recently electricity from fossil fuels was far cheaper than electricity from renewables. This has dramatically changed within the last decade. In most places in the world power from new renewables is now cheaper than power from new fossil fuels.

entry.title: Our World in Data switches to Johns Hopkins University as our main data source for COVID-19 cases and deaths
entry.summary: To continue providing daily updates on the Coronavirus Pandemic, Our World in Data has switched from ECDC to Johns Hopkins University as our data source for confirmed cases and deaths.

entry.title: Breaking out of the Malthusian trap: How pandemics allow us to understand why our ancestors were stuck in poverty
entry.summary: For much of human history there was no escape from poverty. Our ancestors were trapped in an economy in which incomes were determined by the size of the population. Productivity gains did not raise living standards, but instead translated to increases of the population size, which left everyone as badly off as before. This mechanism that kept our ancestors in poverty is referred to as the ‘Malthusian Trap’. The Industrial Revolution ended the Malthusian economy and made it possible for a country to leave abject poverty behind.

entry.title: Why do COVID-19 deaths in Sweden always appear to decrease in the last 10 days?
entry.summary: There are two ways that COVID-19 deaths can be presented over time: by the date of death, or the date on which the death is reported. The data for Sweden is shown by date of death – this means the most recent points should be treated as incomplete.

entry.title: Where in the world do people have the highest CO2 emissions from flying?
entry.summary: Globally, aviation accounts for around 2.5% of CO2 emissions. But for many, it accounts for a much larger share. Where in the world do people have the highest emissions from flying?

entry.title: Less than half of young Americans voted in the last presidential election
entry.summary: 40% of eligible voters in the US did not vote in the 2016 election. Young Americans in particular often do not exercise their right to vote – less than half of eligible under-30s voted in the last election.

entry.title: Why is life expectancy in the US lower than in other rich countries?
Short-haul vs. long-haul; rich vs. poor countries: where do global CO2 emissions from aviation come from?
entry.summary: Aviation accounts for 2.5% of global CO2 emissions. How much from short vs. long-haul flights? How much from rich vs. poor countries?

entry.title: Climate change and flying: what share of global CO2 emissions come from aviation?
entry.summary: Flying is a highly controversial topic in climate debates. It accounts for around 2.5% of global CO2 emissions, but 3.5% when we take non-CO2 impacts on climate into account.

entry.title: Which form of transport has the smallest carbon footprint?
entry.summary: Transport accounts for almost one-quarter of global CO2 emissions. For individuals, it can be the largest part of their carbon footprint. But if you need to travel, what is the lowest-carbon way to do so? We take a look at the data.
